concepts.title: Definition of composite function
concepts.conceptBody:
Composite Functions

Let us consider two functions, $\text{f}:\text{X}\to {{\text{Y}}_{1}}$ and $\text{g}:{{\text{Y}}_{1}}\to \text{Y}$. We define function $\text{h}:\text{X}\to \text{Y}$ such that $h\left( x \right)=g\left( f\left( x \right) \right)$. To obtain $h\left( x \right)$, we first take the f-image of an element $x\in X$ so that $f\left( x \right)\in {{Y}_{1}}$, which is the domain of $g\left( x \right)$. Then take g-image of $f\left( x \right)$, i.e. $g(f\left( x \right)$ i.e. $g\left( f\left( x \right) \right)$ which would be an element of $\text{Y}$. The adjacent figure clearly shows the steps to be taken.

The function ' $\text{h}$ ' defined above is called the composition of $\text{f}$ and $\text{g}$ and is denoted by gof.

Thus (gof) $\text{x}=\text{g}(\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$. Clearly Domain (gof) $=\{\text{x}:\text{x}\in $ Domain (f), $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\in $ Domain $\left( \text{g} \right)\}$ Similarly we can define, $\left( \text{fog} \right)\text{x}=\text{f}\left( \text{g}\left( \text{x} \right) \right)$ and Domain $\left( \text{fog} \right)=\{\text{x}:\text{x}\in $ Domain $\left( \text{g} \right),\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ Domain $\left( \text{f} \right)\}$. In general fog $\ne \text{g}$ of.

Explanation : 

(i) To understand the concept of complete function consider fog (x) :

in the above diagram for Ist block ' $\text{x}$ ' is the independent variable and

corresponding $\text{g}\left( \text{x} \right)$ is the dependent variable. But for IIrd block $\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)$ i.e. the

dependent variable of Ist block is independent variable of the IInd block and

corresponding fog $\left( \text{x} \right)$ is the dependent variable of IInd block.

concepts.title: Finding composite functions
concepts.conceptBody:
General steps for determining composite functions 

Step 1 : Find critical points

(a) Draw graph of first block.

(b) Draw y $=\text{k}$ (horizontal lines)

$\text{k}\in $ critical pt(s) for second block

(c) Make pt(s) of intersection & find corresponding values of $\text{x}$.

(d) Critical pt(s) of first block & values obtained in c are critical pt(s) of composite function.

Step 2 : Divide interval about critical point.

Step 3 : In each and every interval find appropriate definition of the function.

concepts.title: Solved Problems based on compsite function
concepts.conceptBody:
Solved Problems based on compsite function

Consider the function as defined as under 

Evaluate f $\left[ f\left( x \right) \right]$ 

Solution : 

Here we have to evaluate fof $\left( \text{x} \right)$

 

According to the rules that we have mentioned above,

Step 1 : It says draw the graph of first block.

i.e. $f\left( x \right)$

we know $f\left( x \right)\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
1+x & ;0\le x\le 2 \\
3-x & ;2<x\le 3 \\
\end{array} \right\}$

(b) Now we have to $\text{y}=\text{k}$ where $\text{k}\in $ critical points of the above function which

is 2 in this question.

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ draw $y=2$ line & find the point of intersection, which comes out to be 1

(c) now the critical point found will be the critical point of the composite function.

Also the critical point of first block will add to the critical points of composite function.

Step 2 : For composite function; now

the interval will be divided as follows

 

$0\le \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\le 2\And $

$2<\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right)\le 3$

$\And $ now seeing for values, for these values.

Step 3 : Now finding appropriate values for their intervals

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
{} & \text{f}\left[ \text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) \right]=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
1+\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) & 0\le \text{x}\le 1 \\
3-\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) & 1<\text{x}\le 2 \\
1+\text{f}\left( \text{x} \right) & 2<\text{x}\le 3 \\
\end{array} \right\} \\
{} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ as }\!\!~\!\!\text{ can }\!\!~\!\!\text{ be }\!\!~\!\!\text{ seen }\!\!~\!\!\text{ in }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ above }\!\!~\!\!\text{ graph}\text{. }\!\!~\!\!\text{ } \\
{} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ Now }\!\!~\!\!\text{ put }\!\!~\!\!\text{ values }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ corresponding }\!\!~\!\!\text{ to }\!\!~\!\!\text{ the }\!\!~\!\!\text{ interval }\!\!~\!\!\text{ of }\!\!~\!\!\text{ x}. \\
{} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ fof }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
1+\left( 1+\text{x} \right) & 0\le \text{x}\le 1 \\
3-\left( 1+\text{x} \right) & 1<\text{x}\le 2 \\
1+\left( 3-\text{x} \right) & 2<\text{x}\le 3 \\
\end{array} \right\} \\
{} & ~\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ fof }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
2+\text{x} & 0\le \text{x}\le 1 \\
2-\text{x} & 1<\text{x}\le 2 \\
4-\text{x} & 2<\text{x}\le 3 \\
\end{array} \right\} \\
\end{array}$

Q) Evaluate and draw the graph of following functions : 

(a) $f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}x \right)$ (b) $f\left( x \right)=\text{sin}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x \right)$ 

Solution : 

(a) $f\left( x \right)=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}x \right)$

This can be considered a composite function with $\text{f}\left( \text{g}\left( \text{x} \right) \right)$ as $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}\left( \text{x} \right) \right)$

It is clear that ${{D}_{f}}\in R$, since $\text{sin}x$ is valid for all values of $x\And $ it value ranges from -1

to +1 which satisfies $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}$ function also.

We know the graph of $\text{y}=\text{sinx}$ from we get critical points as $\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{3\pi }{2}\ldots \left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$ (We

will cover critical points later also).

For now you can say that in such curves the points on which the tangent is parallel to x-

axis are the critical points.

$\begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }0\le x\le \frac{\pi }{2} & \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}x \right)=x \\
\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ for }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{2}\le x\le \frac{3\pi }{2} & \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}x \right)=\pi -x \\
\end{array}$

Now this is important to understand this point, whenever we use inverse functions we

represent them in their principal value branch.

$\And \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$ principal value branch is $\left[ -\frac{\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right]$ so for value between $\left( \frac{\pi }{2},\frac{3\pi }{2} \right]$ we have

to convert them into principal values, which we do by subtracting it from $\pi $.

So similarly for interval $\frac{3\pi }{2}<x<\frac{5\pi }{2}$, we will subtract them by $2\pi $ and so on.

So the final graph becomes

\(f(x)=\sin ^{-1}(\sin x)= \begin{cases}x & ;-\frac{\pi}{2} \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2} \\ \pi-x & ; \frac{\pi}{2}<x \leq \frac{3 \pi}{2} \\ x-2 \pi & ; \frac{3 \pi}{2}<x<\frac{5 \pi}{2}\end{cases}\)

(b) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{sin}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} \right)$

first of all we will find the domain of the function for $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x},\text{x}$ can only take values between $-1\And 1$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{D}}_{\text{f}}}\in \left[ -1,1 \right]$

$\And $ since values between $-1\And 1$ lie between $\frac{-\pi }{2}\And \frac{\pi }{2}$ (i.e. the principal value branch)

$\text{sin}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} \right)=\text{x}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ f}\left( \text{x} \right)=\text{sin}\left( \text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} \right)=\text{x};-1\le \text{x}\le 1$

Q) Draw the following graphs : 

(a) $y=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{tan}x \right)$ 

(b) $y=\text{sec}\left( \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x \right)$ 

Solution : 

(a) given $\text{y}=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{tanx} \right)$

Again here, first of all we will try to find out the domain of the function

We know $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$ will be valid for all $\text{x}\in \text{R}$

$\And \text{tan}x$ returns real values for $x\in R-\left( 2n+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{D}}_{\text{f}}}\in \text{R}-\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$

& Range will be according to the outer function which is $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}$ here

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{R}}_{\text{f}}}\in \left( \frac{-\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

We know the graph of $y=\text{tan}x$

 

Which is discontinuous at $\text{x}\in \left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$

so $\left( 2\text{n}+1 \right)\frac{\pi }{2}$ for $\text{n}=0,1,2\ldots $

become its critical points.

so for $\frac{-\pi }{2}<\text{x}<\frac{\pi }{2}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{tanx} \right)=\text{x}$

$\And $ for $\frac{\pi }{2}<x<\frac{3\pi }{2}\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{tan}x \right)=x-\pi $

again according to principal value branch which $\left( \frac{-\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ for $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$.

 

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{tan}x \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
\text{x}+\pi & \frac{-3\pi }{2}<\text{x}<\frac{-\pi }{2} \\
\text{x} & \frac{-\pi }{2}<\text{x}<\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\text{x}-\pi & \frac{\pi }{2}<\text{x}<\frac{3\pi }{2} \\
\end{array} \right\}$

Note $:{{(2\text{n}+1)}_{\frac{\pi }{2}}}$ points are not included as they are not part of domain.

(b) $y=\text{sec}\left( \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}x \right)$

we know the graph of $\text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

${{\text{D}}_{\text{f}}}\in \left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$

 

${{\text{R}}_{\text{f}}}\in \left[ 0,\pi \right]-\left\{ \frac{\pi }{2} \right\}$

and for this range the sec function is also valid.

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{D}}_{\text{f}}}\in \left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$

$\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{R}_{f}}\in \left( -\infty ,-1\left] \cup \right[1,\infty \right)$ (as equal to the range of normal sec $\text{x}$ function)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\text{sec}\left( \text{se}{{\text{c}}^{-1}}\text{x} \right)=\text{x}$ (for the given domain)

Note : The fundamental of principal value branch comes only when the outer function is an

inverse function because it is the property of inverse functions only.

So, drawing the graph now

Draw the following graphs : 

(a) $y=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2x}{1+{{x}^{2}}} \right)$ (b) $y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{1-{{x}^{2}}}{1+{{x}^{2}}} \right)$ 

Solution : (a) given, $y=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2\text{x}}{1+{{\text{x}}^{2}}} \right)$

These kind of problems are solved by substitution by putting $\text{x}=\text{tan}\theta \And \theta \in \left( \frac{-\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$

the principal branch value for tan

 

$\begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
{} & \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ we }\!\!~\!\!\text{ get }\!\!~\!\!\text{ si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{2\text{tan}\theta }{1+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta } \right)\left[ \text{sin}ce\frac{2\text{tan}\theta }{1+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta }=\text{sin}2\theta \right] \\
{} & ~=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}2\theta \right) \\
\end{array}$

So, now the function becomes

$y=\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sin}2\theta \right)$

but we know the graph of $\text{si}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\left( \text{sinx} \right)$ i.e. $\text{y}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-\text{x}+\pi \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{-3\pi }{2}\le \text{x}<\frac{-\pi }{2} \\
\text{x} \\
\frac{-\pi }{2}\le \text{x}<\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\pi -\text{x }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{2}\le \text{x}<\frac{3\pi }{2} \\
\end{array} \right\}$ replacing $\text{x}$ by $2\theta $
replacing $\text{x}$ by $2\theta $
$\begin{matrix}
\text{y}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\theta +\pi & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{-3\pi }{2}\le 2\theta <\frac{-\pi }{2} \\
2\theta & ;\frac{-\pi }{2}\le 2\theta <\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\pi -2\theta & ;\frac{\pi }{2}\le 2\theta \le \frac{3\pi }{2} \\
\end{array} \right\} \\
\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\theta +\pi & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{-3\pi }{4}\le \theta \le \frac{-\pi }{4} \\
2\theta & ;-\frac{\pi }{4}\le \theta <\pi /4 \\
\pi -2\theta & ;\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\pi }{4}\le \theta <\frac{3\pi }{4} \\
\end{array} \right\} \\
\end{matrix}$
and we substitute $\text{x}=\text{tan}\theta $
$\Rightarrow \theta =\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}\And \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ also }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\theta \in \left( \frac{-\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$
replacing the value of $\theta $ in above values
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}+\pi & ;-\frac{\pi }{2}<\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}\le \frac{-\pi }{4} \\
2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;-\frac{\pi }{4}\le \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}<\frac{\pi }{4} \\
\pi -2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;\frac{\pi }{4}\le \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}<\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\end{array} \right\}$
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}+\pi & ;\text{tan}\left( -\frac{\pi }{2} \right)<\text{x}<\text{tan}\left( -\frac{\pi }{4} \right) \\
2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;\text{tan}\left( \frac{-\pi }{4} \right)\le \text{x}<\text{tan}\left( \frac{\pi }{4} \right) \\
\pi -2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;\text{tan}\frac{\pi }{4}\le \text{x}<\text{tan}\left( \frac{\pi }{2} \right) \\
\end{array} \right\}$
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}+\pi & ;-\infty <\text{x}<-1 \\
2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;-1\le \text{x}<1 \\
\pi -2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x} & ;1\le \text{x}<\infty \\
\end{array} \right\}$
Now we will draw the graph of the final function we know the graph of $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$, so
drawing the final graph from it.

 so the final graph is

(b) $y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \frac{1-{{x}^{2}}}{1+{{x}^{2}}} \right)$

here putting $\text{x}=\text{tan}\theta $

 

$\And \theta \in \left( \frac{-\pi }{2},\frac{\pi }{2} \right)$ {make a rule to write down constraint as they come
$\frac{1-\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta }{1+\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{2}}\theta }=\text{cos}2\theta $
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \text{cos}2\theta \right)$
$y=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \text{cos}x \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-x & ,-\pi \le x<0 \\
x & ,0\le x\le \pi \\
\end{array} \right.$
for those who do not know this graph, try to solve it on your own.
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\text{co}{{\text{s}}^{-1}}\left( \text{cos}\left( 2\theta \right) \right)=\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{l}}
-2\theta & ;-\pi \le 2\theta \le 0 \\
2\theta & ;0\le 2\theta <\pi \\
\end{array} \right.$
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\left\{ \begin{matrix}
-2\theta & ;\frac{-\pi }{2}\le \theta <0 \\
2\theta & ;0\le \theta <\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\end{matrix} \right\}$
replacing $\theta$ by $\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}$

 

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\left\{ \begin{array}{*{35}{r}}
-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}; & -\frac{\pi }{2}\le \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}<0 \\
2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}; & 0\le \text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}\text{x}<\frac{\pi }{2} \\
\end{array} \right\}$
$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }y=\left\{ \begin{matrix}
-2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x;-\infty \le x\le 0 \\
2\text{ta}{{\text{n}}^{-1}}x;0<x<\infty \\
\end{matrix} \right\}$
$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Final graph is
